nodes.text: You look like you haven't slept. Again.

choices.label: Ask about the note on "the arrangement below."
What do we do if the debt breaks us?
Talk about the house's growing reputation.

nodes.text: Marion studies you. 'As you say, my lord.' She doesn't believe you.
She nods slowly. 'Then let me carry some of it.' Her hand rests near yours for a moment, then she steps back.

Marion takes the chit and goes still. "This is one of ours. Two ledgers pulled the night the house was stripped — not stolen at random. Chosen." She taps the quartermaster mark with one nail. "Someone knew exactly which pages mattered."

choices.label: Then the debt was handled before it ever reached me.

outcome.value: The bureau chit proves the ledgers were selected, not looted at random. The debt may have been shaped before it reached your name.

nodes.text: Marion reads the fragment twice. "'The arrangement below' wasn't poetry." She looks toward the cellar stairs. "Your father never trusted a single lock when a hidden catch would do. If this note is real, the vault didn't need a key first. It needed the right clue."

choices.label: Then we read the house before we force it.

outcome.value: Marion believes the vault opens by hidden release, not by key alone. The house is meant to be read like a ledger.

nodes.text: Marion looks up from a ledger. She's been running numbers since before you woke. She hasn't offered any of it to you yet.
She turns the ledger around. Three columns: what you owe, what's coming in, what the gap is. The gap is large. She taps it once. "You need a job done before the week is out."

choices.label: I know how to run a household.
Who kept the original accounts before all this?

nodes.text: "Don't thank me. Get the job done." She takes back the ledger. It is the closest she comes to a compliment this early.
She holds your gaze for a moment. Then she closes the ledger and sets it back in front of herself. "As you say." She does not offer the numbers again.

Marion's expression shutters for a moment. "Orren Wex. He kept the original debt books. The Compact took him six weeks ago and buried him in the Hollows under quiet custody." She lowers her voice. "If anyone can prove the tally was bent before it reached your name, it's Orren."

choices.label: Then I need to get to him before they make him disappear.

outcome.value: quest-house-fall-reckoning

choices.label: Not yet. I need more room to move first.

nodes.text: Marion is at the window. She doesn't turn when you enter. After a moment: "I've been thinking about what we do if it comes to the worst."

She turns. "The creditors take the house. You scatter. Everyone goes their own way." She folds her arms. "I have a list of what we sell first. And a route out of Valdenmoor that avoids the Compact checkpoints." She pauses. "I'm not saying we use it. I'm saying I have it."
She nods once. She turns back to the window. Whatever she had planned stays unspoken. But you get the sense she's already done the thinking — she just won't burden you with it yet.
"Good." She unfolds her arms. "Then stop looking at the floor and do something." It is, you realize, encouragement.
Marion looks at you for a long moment. "No," she says quietly. "It might." She doesn't press it. The route stays in her head. She hopes she never needs it.
Marion sets a cup of tea in front of you without being asked. She does this rarely. "There are people in the market who step aside when I say the house name," she says. "It's new."

choices.label: Then we need to be careful. Visibility cuts both ways.

nodes.text: Marion picks up her own cup. "Yes," she says. She does not often say things like this plainly. "We did." She looks out the window at the street below. The cup is warm in both their hands.

Marion looks at you with something that is not quite surprise. "Yes." She sits down. "I've already started thinking about who might want to test it." She opens the ledger to a different page — names, not numbers. "I'll keep watching."
Marion considers this. "It matters because people behave differently around names they respect." She pauses. "Or fear. We should decide which we want to be." She does not push you to answer now.
Marion finds you before supper. She has a question — you can tell by the way she's standing, hands clasped behind her back. "Can I ask you something that isn't about the household?"

She takes a breath. "Do you actually want this? The house, the debt, all of it. Or did you just find yourself here and haven't found a reason to leave yet?" She is watching your face closely. She will know if you lie.

choices.label: I want it. I'm just not sure how yet.

nodes.text: Marion nods. "Of course." She does not ask again. The question will wait. She hopes the answer will still be worth hearing when you're ready.
Marion is quiet for a moment. "Good," she says finally. "That's the right answer. The ones who are certain usually aren't." She unclasp her hands. Something in her has settled. "Supper in an hour."

She considers this for a long moment. "Most people are where they are because they found themselves there." She looks out toward the front door. "The difference is what they do about it." She leaves the question open. You know which side of it she's on.
Marion catches your eye across the room. There's something different in her expression today — less duty, more choice.
She sets down her pen. "I've been thinking about what we are to each other. Not house and sworn sword. Just... us." She hesitates — rare for her. "Is there nothing between us but duty?"

choices.label: I haven't let myself think about it. But I want to.
We have enough to worry about.
There's more. I've just been... careful.

nodes.text: Marion nods slowly. "Careful is wise." She picks up her pen again, but her hand is less steady than before. "Just don't let caution become cowardice." She doesn't look up, but her voice is softer.
"I want to know if you see me." She sets down her ledger. "Not the role I play. Not the sword I carry. Just... me."

choices.label: I see you. More than I let on.
I'm not sure I know who that is yet.

nodes.text: For the first time, Marion Vale looks uncertain. "Then tell me something true," she says. "Something you haven't said to anyone else." She's not asking for confessions. She's asking for trust.

Marion listens without interrupting. When you're done, she exhales slowly. "I'm afraid too." She says it like a confession. "Not of dying. Of failing you." She looks up. "But I'd rather fail trying than never try at all."
Something in Marion's posture softens. "I hope for this," she says quietly. "Us. Not as house and sword. As... something chosen." She pauses. "When there's time. When it's safe."
Marion nods. "Honesty is a start." She doesn't push further today. But she looks at you differently — like she's waiting to see if you'll follow through.
Marion's expression closes off. "Of course." She picks up her pen again. "There's always something." But the warmth is gone from her voice.
"Careful is wise," Marion says. "But don't let caution become cowardice." She sets down her pen. "When you're ready to stop being careful, I'll be here."
Verek Holst has already chosen the seat facing the door, same as always. "What'll it be? News costs the same as ale around here."

outcome.value: collectors-asking

nodes.text: He leans in, and for a moment his mouth moves through half a statute number before he catches himself -- old habit, old job. "Collectors were asking about the old Valdris accounts last night."

Garet Doyle leans across the bar, wiping a glass that hasn't been clean in years. "House Valdris. You're still standing, then." He's already decided what to call you in his head -- he never uses real names until they've paid twice. "What'll it be?"

choices.label: What's in the locked box behind the bar?

nodes.text: "Compact's been asking questions about the old families. Not the kind of asking where they want answers. The kind where they already have them." He sets the glass down. "You didn't hear it here."

Garet's hand stops on the glass. "Old things. Service tokens. Ring fragments. A few writs nobody's paid in years." He doesn't reach for it. "All from your house, before the Breach. I've been waiting for someone who'd know what to do with them without making me responsible for whatever happens next."

choices.label: I'll take that responsibility, when I'm ready.

outcome.value: Garet Doyle keeps a locked box of pre-Breach Valdris relics behind the bar -- service tokens, ring fragments, unpaid writs. He's waiting for you to decide what happens to them.

nodes.text: Lira Ashcroft doesn't look up from the scale she's adjusting. "Goods or gossip. I don't deal in anything else." She finally meets your eye. "Which is it?"

choices.label: I have something to move quietly.
You never talk about where you're from.

nodes.text: "Bring it by the stall tomorrow. No names, no ledger." She slides a card across the table -- a time and a dock number. "Don't be late. I'm not sentimental."
"Information costs more than goods." She names a price with her eyes. "What's it worth to you?"

Lira doesn't raise her voice. She never does -- not even now, and this is the closest anything has come to it. "I haven't been back to the old district in years." A long pause. "I'm not going to start explaining why today."

Bog doesn't waste words. He offers a handshake before anything else -- firm, testing, the way he tests every stranger who wants dock work. Whatever he reads there decides the rest of the conversation. "Speak."

choices.label: I need hands for the morning shift.

nodes.text: "Three Marks a head, up front." He's already running names in his head -- who's on rotation, who owes him a shift, who can be spared. "They work, they get paid. They don't work, they don't come back." He holds out the same hand again, palm up. "Simple as that."
There's a figure in the corner that you almost missed. "I know why you're here," The Wren says. No greeting. No name. "The question is whether what I have is worth what you'll pay."

choices.label: What do you know about the Pale Sisters?

nodes.text: "They move assets through the Harbor on the second and fourth nights of each month. What those assets are, I won't say here." The Wren slides a folded paper across the table. "Read it. Burn it."

outcome.value: item-papers-false-citizen

nodes.text: The old woman looks at you with the particular clarity of the very old. 'Valdris,' she says softly. 'I knew your grandfather. He made a promise he didn't keep. I'm waiting to see if it skips a generation.'

choices.label: Show her the crest ring from the house.
I don't have time for old stories.

nodes.text: 'He said if it ever happened again, the family would be there. Be ready.' She looks out the window at something you can't see. 'The Breach wasn't the first. He knew that. I think you're starting to understand it too.'

outcome.value: Old Maret suggests the Breach may not have been the first — and your grandfather knew.

nodes.text: Old Maret takes the ring between two fingers and turns it toward the light. "River office work," she says at last. "Gilded Court money with Compact habits. Not old blood — hired hands who dressed themselves up to look ancestral." She presses it back into your palm. "Someone respectable wanted Mira to trust the wrong crest."

choices.label: Then the house was approached, not merely raided.

outcome.value: Old Maret ties the crest ring to Gilded Court river factors using Compact intermediaries. Mira was courted before the house was broken.

nodes.text: Sister Vael glances up from a stack of tightly-folded notes. "House Valdris. I've been wondering when you'd come." She folds her hands. "Remedies or rumours?"

choices.label: One of my people needs attention.
Why do you really do this work?

nodes.text: She lights a second candle before she answers -- a habit she's never explained. "The Compact has been moving injured men out of sight, quietly, from the Detention House. They're not criminals." She blows out the second candle the instant she's said it. "Whatever is happening beyond the gates is closer than anyone is saying." And then she is already moving toward the door -- she never stays past the point where she's said her piece.
"Send them by tonight. I'll see what I can do. I don't charge the Pale folk what I charge the Heights." She writes something on a slip of paper. "Give them this."

Sister Vael doesn't light a candle this time -- she blows one out instead. "I had a sister. The temple erased her from its own records after the Breach, smuggled out under plague blankets by someone whose name I have been hunting since." She looks at you evenly. "I don't do this for penance. I do it so no one else's sister disappears from a book and stays disappeared."

choices.label: I'll help you find the name if I can.

nodes.text: The woman in the safe house studies you before she speaks. "Marion was right," she says. "You still come looking like the house is on fire, even when you're trying to play lord." She lets the old name hang between you. Tessaly Ash. Alive. Waiting.

choices.label: Orren said you know where Mira was taken.

nodes.text: "Taken isn't the word," Tessaly says. "Moved, hidden, watched, and nearly traded. The Court keeps her in the old tannery on the Pale's eastern edge. If you go, go hard and go now. They haven't decided what she's worth yet."

choices.label: Then put the lead in my hand.

outcome.value: quest-mira-rescue

nodes.text: Tessaly does not touch the envelope at first. "She kept that?" The question is quieter than the room. After a moment she exhales. "Then she was trying to leave you a trail. Good. That means she thought you'd still be alive to follow it."

choices.label: I need the rest of the trail.
Then I'll come back when I can move on it.

nodes.text: Ida Rhys looks up from her kit, takes your measure in about two seconds, and waits. She is not unfriendly. She is simply not going to fill the silence for you.

choices.label: What do you think of the work so far?

nodes.text: 'The work is honest. The house is new.' A pause. 'That's not a complaint. New houses don't have old loyalties in the way.' She goes back to her kit.

choices.label: Do you have old loyalties I should know about?

nodes.text: She sets down what she's holding. 'That's a house question, not a personal one. I'd have said something if it mattered.' She picks it back up. The conversation is over.

outcome.value: Ida Rhys: no prior loyalties declared. She said what needed saying.

nodes.text: Ida looks up as you approach. She doesn't put down her work, but her gaze lingers a beat longer than professional courtesy requires.

choices.label: You're good at what you do.

nodes.text: 'I know.' No modesty. No false denial. Just acknowledgment. She tests the edge of her blade. 'You're not bad yourself.' There's something almost playful in her tone.
Ida smirks. 'Don't get cocky.' But there's no heat in it. 'I've seen better. You're... promising.' She sheathes her blade. 'That's more than I say to most.'
'I don't give praise lightly.' She wipes down her kit with deliberate strokes. 'If I'm working with you, I'd better trust you won't get me killed.' She looks up. 'So far, you haven't.'
Ida sets down her work. 'Time's always got somewhere to go. Question is whether it's worth spending.' She watches you. 'What do you want, house lord?'

choices.label: I want to know the person behind the contract.
Just checking in. See how you're doing.

nodes.text: Ida goes still. Then: 'That's not a question I get often.' She considers. 'I'm good at my work because I have to be. Not because I chose it.' She meets your gaze. 'There's a difference.'

choices.label: What would you choose, if you could?

nodes.text: Ida looks away. 'Something where I don't have to calculate survival every breath.' She looks back. 'But that's a luxury. And luxuries cost.' A pause. 'Maybe I'm starting to think you're worth the cost.'
'Do you?' Ida studies you. 'Then you know what it's like to have no room for mistakes.' She picks up her work again. 'Good. Then you won't ask me for things I can't give.'
Ida considers this. 'I'm fine.' A beat. 'Better than fine.' She doesn't elaborate, but something in her posture has softened. 'Thanks for asking.'
Mira is quiet for longer than is comfortable. When she speaks, it is careful. "I want to know what the house needs from me. Before anything else. That's not ingratitude. That's how I survived."

choices.label: Tell me what you know about the Court's arrangement.

nodes.text: She nods. Just once. "All right." There is something in the way she says it that suggests she did not expect that answer.

She doesn't answer at first. "Forty-one days," she says instead. "That's how long since I saw open sky, before they let me onto the tannery roof once, in the dark, so I wouldn't run." Only then, quieter: "They didn't want me for information. They wanted me as a record."
Mira's jaw tightens. She gives you the shape of it -- a Court record, nothing more -- and stops there. Whatever else there was to tell, she keeps.

outcome.value: Mira answered only the least of what she knows -- pressed too soon.

nodes.text: She unfolds slowly, on her own time. "They didn't want me for information. They wanted me as a record -- something they could produce if the debt dispute moved into formal channels." A longer pause than before. "There is a letter. I kept it. I don't know if it changes anything."

outcome.value: Mira mentions a letter -- the Court kept her as a document, not leverage.

nodes.text: Orren Wex looks like a man still counting things up. "I owe you an explanation and a ledger entry," he says. "Which would you prefer first?"

"The original debt entry was altered after it was recorded. Not erased — altered. The kind of change that looks legitimate to anyone who does not know what the original said." He spreads his hands. "I know what it said."

outcome.value: Orren Wex: the debt entry was altered post-recording. He knows the original figures.

nodes.text: "I stayed quiet because I thought quiet was safer. For me, and for the house. I was wrong about that — six weeks in a Compact holding room proved it." He pauses. "I am not wrong about the debt. The numbers were changed."

Torvald Messe keeps his copy of the ledger close. Not hidden -- close. There's a difference. "Filing Statute 44 dash 7," he says first, out of habit, before anything else. "Now: I know what you're going to ask. Ask it properly and we'll see."

choices.label: I need to see the copy.
Not yet. I wanted you to know we know it exists.

nodes.text: "The copy exists because someone anticipated the original would be tampered with." He sets three identical folios on the table, though only one matters today. "My father lost everything to a single forged paper. I don't deal in single papers anymore." He studies you. "The house should be equally careful about how it uses this."
He takes that in. "Knowing the house knows means you're not fishing." A small nod. "Come back when you need it. I'll have it ready -- in triplicate, as always."

outcome.value: Torvald Messe will hold the ledger copy until the house needs it.

nodes.text: Orven Pell doesn't greet you by name. When he finally speaks, he starts, as always, with a date. "Filed the ninth of Ashfall, the year of the Breach." Only then does he look up. "If you know who I am, you know what I'm holding. If Merrow knows the same, this conversation has a clock on it."
"They know the ledger exists. They do not know where. That will change." He does not elaborate on when.

outcome.value: Orven Pell: Merrow knows the ledger exists but not its location. Time is limited.

nodes.text: "Filed the ninth of Ashfall." He says it again, like a citation. "Names and transactions Merrow has spent considerable effort keeping out of the Register. Some names are Compact. Some are Gilded Court." He pauses. "I know which ledgers burned that week, and which merely vanished. This one vanished. That's worse for Merrow."

Brannic Thule watches you the way a man watches the door. "My lord," he says -- he has never once gotten the address wrong, not from the first day. "I was told testifying would be protected. I'm still waiting to see what protected looks like."

choices.label: The house will stand behind you.

nodes.text: He takes a breath. "All right. Because I don't have better options." He straightens. "Ask your questions, my lord. I'll answer what I saw."

"I saw the transaction. I saw who countersigned it. I know the signature was not Orren Wex's." He meets your eyes for the first time. "I was steward's assistant in what you'd call the receiving hall -- I still call it that, though it's been an armory for years now. I have been waiting for someone to ask."

choices.label: We need that formally. Will you do it?

outcome.value: Brannic Thule witnessed the altered countersignature. He is willing to testify.

nodes.text: Dalen Morke makes conversation sound like negotiation, though his eyes are shadowed in a way negotiation doesn't explain. "House Valdris," he says, as if appraising something. "The name still opens certain doors. Fewer than it did. What brings you to this one?"

choices.label: The debt dispute. We believe the original record was falsified.
We're looking for allies who understand what the Court does when it moves quietly.

nodes.text: "I'm sorry," he says first -- he always says it first, before the rest arrives. "Falsified records are interesting only if you can prove it before the Court moves on them. After that, they become historical facts." He smooths his sleeve. "Do you have a timeline?"

outcome.value: Dalen Morke: timing matters. The case must be made before the Court formalizes the debt.

nodes.text: "That kind of understanding has a cost. Not necessarily coin." He looks at you for a moment longer than is comfortable -- longer than he's slept, probably. "Come back when you have something to offer."

Sable Wrent doesn't waste words. "New contract, new house. Same work." She's already counting something on her fingers -- favours owed, maybe, though she's never once written one down. "I've got questions if you've got answers. Otherwise tell me where I'm needed."

choices.label: What do you need to know?
You'll get your orders when there's something to do.

nodes.text: "Payment schedule. Rules of engagement. Who I report to when the house is not around." She waits. "The last outfit I worked for" -- she doesn't name it, she never names it -- "didn't answer the third one until it was a problem."

choices.label: You report to the house. Payment on settlement. No unnecessary force.

nodes.text: "Right." She nods once, reading something in that response, and marks it down in the tally only she can see. "I'll be ready."
Cessa Rill doesn't look up from the ledger of names. "Baptisms, adoptions, deaths. That's what I keep. If you're here about something else, you're in the wrong chapel room."

choices.label: You've kept these records a long time. Ever make one you couldn't undo?

nodes.text: Cessa goes very still, the way she does when a name is wrong and she is deciding whether to say so. "Once. Years ago. I altered a birth entry to save a child from an inheritance law that would have destroyed her by paperwork alone -- not anyone you'd know, not anyone with a house name." She corrects nothing about how she says it, which is its own kind of admission. "I have never known if that was mercy or theft. I don't expect to, before I die."

choices.label: You saved her. That has to count for something.

nodes.text: Lady Sorn asks a question she plainly already knows the answer to. "And how is the debt settling, House Valdris?" She doesn't wait for surprise. "I ask the ones I already know the answer to. It tells me whether you'll lie to someone who can check."
Something that might be approval crosses her face. "Good. I remember the names of everyone's dead, House Valdris. I'd rather not add yours to a list I keep for people who wasted my time."
"Well enough," she repeats, testing the words. "That's a Court answer. I collect those too."

choices.label: It's the only one I have today.

nodes.text: Lirien Ashcroft marks the date on her wrist in ink before she even looks up -- she does it every morning, the way some people check the weather. "You're the house Mother mentioned," she says. "She thinks I don't listen at the stall. I listen to everything."

choices.label: Your mother trusts me. Do you?

nodes.text: Lirien is quiet for a moment. "She doesn't tell me things. Not the important ones." Her hand moves toward her wrist, then stops. "I don't know yet if I'm her heiress or just the evidence of something she'd rather forget. Maybe it doesn't matter which."
Ser Veyran Malk thanks you for your time before he's said anything else -- a courtesy that costs him nothing and warns you everything. "House Valdris. I trust the accounts are in order."

choices.label: We know the debt was shaped before it reached us.

nodes.text: He doesn't raise his voice. He never has to. "That would be an interesting claim to make publicly." A pause, precisely timed. "I don't repeat a warning. Once is sufficient."

Alis Vey speaks of her house in the past tense, without affect, the way she speaks of everything from before. "House Valdris. My family was a cadet ally of yours, once. I sold your property under coercion during the Breach." She says it plainly, like a filed fact, not a confession.

choices.label: What would restitution look like to you?

nodes.text: She keeps a record of every favor she's received since the Breach -- this would be one more line in it, if she let it. "I hold the original transfer documents. The same ones that could void the claim built on my coercion." A pause. "I want to give something back before I confess to holding them. Not the other way around."

choices.label: Then let's find a way to give something back.

outcome.value: Alis Vey holds the original coerced-sale transfer documents that could void Harlen Voss's foreclosure claim -- but wants to make restitution before surrendering them.

nodes.text: Oswin Farr is pricing your boots before he's finished the greeting -- an old habit from six years of running a food line on nothing. "House Valdris. Here about the Warrens, or just passing through?"

choices.label: What does the Warrens actually need?

nodes.text: "Food security that doesn't wait on Compact permission." He produces a folded ration from his coat without thinking -- he always carries emergency rations, has since the shutdown. "Someone's been undercutting my supply arrangements. I have a list of every Compact official who's ever obstructed a delivery. I'm not asking you to fix it. I'm asking you to know it's happening."

choices.label: Noted. I'll keep an eye out.

nodes.text: Tav reads the contract you hand him fully before answering anything, the way he reads every document -- however long it takes. "Walking contract. Limited movement. No family formation rights. Quarterly check-in with the Register." He hands it back. "Ask your question."

choices.label: Are you good in a fight?
This isn't ownership, if that's what worries you.

nodes.text: "I learned by surviving. Not by being taught. That's different." He says it flatly, not looking for sympathy.
Something in him eases, fractionally. "Good. That's the one thing I won't sign under, in any form." He doesn't thank you. But he doesn't leave either.

Maret Sunne corrects a misfiled date on the counter without looking up, then catches herself. "Sorry. Habit." She finally meets your eyes. "House Valdris. What do you need archived -- or un-archived?"

choices.label: Just a question, not a filing.
I hear you keep a very old ledger.

nodes.text: Maret goes very still. "Who told you that." Not quite a question. She doesn't deny it. "I don't know yet what it means, or who I'd become if I used it. I keep a log no one has seen either. Maybe that's a family trait."

choices.label: Whatever it means, you don't have to face it alone.

outcome.value: Maret Sunne holds a ledger proving who profited from House Valdris's fall -- and doesn't yet know she is Old Maret's granddaughter, or that Old Maret knows.

nodes.text: Rutha Kael asks her three questions before you've finished introducing yourself: who's paying, who else knows, what happens if it goes wrong. "Answer those honestly and we'll talk terms."

choices.label: House Valdris. Few people. It's already gone wrong once.

nodes.text: She nods slowly. "Terms that don't change after deployment. I've had that happen once. I'm still cataloguing what it cost."

choices.label: They won't change on you here.

nodes.text: "It matters to me." She doesn't soften it. "I don't work for people who won't answer three questions."

outcome.value: Rutha Kael declined the contract -- she vets every employer with three questions first, no exceptions.

nodes.text: Petra Sunn has already mapped every exit in the room by the time you've sat down -- sound and shadow, before she ever uses a door. "House Valdris. Elyn's mentioned you."

choices.label: The Compact still has a file on you.

nodes.text: Something in Petra's posture loosens, just slightly. "Better than I did, at her age. That's the whole point."

"I know the number," she says flatly. "I found it myself." She doesn't ask how you know. "Six years of bond service. I didn't fight it, because I didn't know you could. Now I make it so other people know they can."
Tessaly Wode holds a small lens up to the light before she answers, measuring you the way she measures a flaw in glass. "Ironworks work, or house work? I don't do both in the same conversation."

choices.label: You trained a dozen engineers who don't credit you for it.

nodes.text: She measures twice before she answers, same as always. "I don't hold it against them. I hold it against the institution that taught them not to." She turns the lens over once. "I'd like to be remembered as someone who built things. Not just someone who was used to."
Tomas Rell doesn't reach for a weapon first. He never does -- it wastes time and leaves witnesses in a bad mood. "House Valdris. I've got a job. You're not making it harder, are you?"
"Then we've got a problem, and I'd rather not have one today." He says it evenly, not as a threat -- as a scheduling complaint.
Catrin Hale checks a signed paper twice before she checks your face once. "Paper doesn't lie to me the way people do. What's your business at this door?"
She counts her keys before answering -- a habit, not a delay. "Names I don't say to people without paper of their own."

outcome.value: Catrin Hale: warder at a deniable holding site. Won't name detainees without proper documentation.

nodes.text: Harlen Voss names the principal before the interest, always -- it's how he was raised to talk about debt. "House Valdris. Let's start with what you actually owe, before we get to what it's grown into."

choices.label: That claim started with a coerced signature.

nodes.text: He doesn't raise his voice -- he never does when threatening. "That would be a very expensive thing to prove." A pause. "Merrow is watching how fast I close this. I'd rather not be slow."

The Dockmaster is already at the boards before the sun is up, same as every morning. He writes your name down twice before he's said a word. "Everyone pays a cut on the morning queue. Coin, silence, or a favor owed. Which is it going to be?"

outcome.value: Paid the Dockmaster's cut in coin.

nodes.text: He doesn't look up from his second copy of the assignment sheet. "Then you don't work these docks. That's fair market rate, the way I run it."

Caevis Sable-Cairn speaks only in formal register, never a contraction, never a colloquialism. "House Valdris. I am given to understand you are managing a considerable debt of your own. We have that, at least, in common."
"I do not name my creditors aloud," he says, unhurried. "I refer to them only as prior obligations. It is a discipline. I recommend it." He does not elaborate further today.
Cress Aldmoor checks your pulse at the wrist before she's finished her greeting -- she does it to everyone who sits close, without thinking. "You're not bleeding. Good. What do you need, then?"

choices.label: You trained under a Court physician.

nodes.text: She doesn't lose count of anything, including this conversation. "I did work I didn't ask about, for silence I gave freely. I left when I couldn't rationalise it anymore." She meets your eyes. "Now I treat anyone who can pay. It's not virtue. It's an improvement over what I was."

choices.label: That's more honest than most confessions.

nodes.text: Dael Morw reads whatever you hand him twice before responding -- a habit from twelve years assessing Register filings. "You'll want to know why a compliance assessor is willing to talk to House Valdris. Fair question."

"A senior assessor once told me to falsify a noble family's birth record. I complied. Then I kept copies and walked out with documents several powerful people would prefer didn't exist." He recites the form number without needing to check. "I am not proud of the first thing. I am counting on the second to matter, when it matters."

choices.label: It might matter sooner than you think.

outcome.value: Dael Morw, a former Compact Register assessor, holds copies of falsified records naming several powerful people -- leverage he has kept for the day it matters.

nodes.text: Evar Koss stores what you tell him by who said it, not by what it was -- he's built that way after twenty years of cargo ledgers. "House Valdris. I take no sides. I take percentages. What's the arrangement?"
"Fair," he repeats, testing the word like currency. "I carry nothing I can't explain if searched. Keep that in mind about whatever you're about to hand me."

Irenne Brek is already watching how you breathe before she's looked at anything else -- she diagnoses by listening, not looking. "You're not hurt. Good. What brings a house lord to a shop with no permit?"

choices.label: Someone in my household needs care.

nodes.text: She's already counting -- she keeps a running tally of everyone she's treated and how they fared. "Send them. I ask no questions about how injuries happen. I never have."

Elyn goes quiet and still when you come in -- not frightened exactly, just watching, the way she does with anything new. She doesn't run. She never runs. She just watches until she's decided something.

choices.label: You don't have to talk if you don't want to.
Is this a place you want to stay?

nodes.text: She considers this for a long moment, the way she considers everything -- only after everyone else has stopped talking. Then, quietly: "Okay." It's the most she's said to you yet.
Elyn is quiet for long enough that you almost think she won't answer. Then: "I don't know yet." A pause. "Petra says I get to decide. I don't know if I believe that yet either."

choices.label: You do get to decide. Take your time.

nodes.text: Bren Aldoth counts exits and headcounts before he relaxes into any room, same as he's done since the unit. "House Valdris. I don't need to know why you need muscle. I need to know if the job comes back on me the way the last one did."
He's quiet for a moment. "Three of my unit didn't come back from the last one that wasn't supposed to go wrong." He doesn't elaborate. "I appreciate you not promising otherwise."

choices.label: You'll know what you're walking into, every time.

nodes.text: Nessa Vain is pricing the replacement cost of your doorframe before you've said a word -- eight years of Compact resource work does that to a person. "House Valdris. I don't ask for permission on small things. I report outcomes instead. I'd like to discuss what counts as small."

choices.label: I trust your judgment on it.

nodes.text: Something settles in her. "Good. I want this house standing after I'm no longer relevant to it. That only works if someone lets me actually build."

Aldric Vane addresses you by full title before anything else, the way he addresses everyone, even the stable hands. "House Valdris. My lord." He's drinking water. He's always drinking water, since the fall.

"A debt collapse. Four years ago." He says it plainly, no self-pity. "I have manners, diction, and no money, and I am acutely aware of what the first two are worth without the third." He sets the water down. "I intend to restore the name. I will not sell the crest to do it."
Dara Slink has already counted the exits in this room before unpacking anything -- a habit from running packages since she was eleven. "Something to move, or just talking?"

"I don't ask what's in the packages. Consider that a virtue." She names a dock and a time. "I've memorised three ways in and out of everywhere between here and the Ironworks boundary. Whatever it is, it'll get there."
Osanna Cray never mentions the Court directly -- always obliquely, always sideways. "You're House Valdris. I used to work somewhere with a similar problem with paperwork." She doesn't say where.

She's quiet for a long moment -- she's already mapped the exits from this room, same as every room she frequents. "Something I shouldn't have. I don't need to describe it. I need to know if it was exceptional, or if it's just how they operate."
Sanna Veld carries only what she can run with -- nothing more, not since the night the house fell and she ran. "House Valdris." She has a prepared answer ready before you've even asked anything.

choices.label: You ran, when the house fell.

nodes.text: The prepared answer doesn't come. For once. "I did. I was loyal, and skilled, and I ran anyway." She looks at you directly. "I'm not asking you to forgive that. I'm asking to not run again."
Brand repeats your greeting back before responding to it, like he's expecting it to change if he waits. "House Valdris." His weapon is clean. His plans, if he has any, are not.

choices.label: What are you waiting for, exactly?

nodes.text: He's quiet long enough that you think he won't answer. "A reason. Any reason." He repeats it, like an order he's testing. "Or permission to stop waiting for one."

Lissel Crane is already reading the room before she says anything -- who's most afraid, who's in charge, who's neither. She's nineteen and has no framework yet for how good her instincts already are. "House Valdris. I don't have anything useful yet. I'm still watching."

She writes something down, the way she always does after a conversation, to read back to herself later. "I keep wondering why people like Petra or Oswin keep doing what costs them everything." She looks up. "I don't want to make the mistake that proves I wasn't ready. So I watch first."

Halvard Senn notes the exits and choke points in the room before anything else about it, twenty-two years of garrison habit. "House Valdris. I want a clear command structure and a clear objective. Tell me you have both."

choices.label: We're still figuring some of it out.

nodes.text: He nods once, satisfied in the way a man is satisfied by a correct answer, not a comforting one. "Then we'll get along."
He recites unit protocol under his breath, an old reflex when observing something not yet running clean. "Then let's fix that before it costs someone."

outcome.value: Halvard Senn: uneasy about unclear command structure. Wants it resolved before it costs someone.

nodes.text: Fenwick Pale is cheerful about brokering berths the port registry would rather not log. "House Valdris! What's moving -- or what needs to not be seen moving?"

choices.label: Name a ship you haven't worked with in years.

nodes.text: He rattles one off without hesitating -- a little memory test he runs on himself, unprompted. Then, switching to past tense the way he does when an arrangement is closing: "That one was a good client. Was." He grins. "My official client list is shorter than my real one, if that answers whatever you were actually asking."

Cutter is sharpening his blade, same time as always, no exceptions -- he'll finish before he answers anything. "Digging through Breach wreckage. Sometimes what I find is worth more as a secret than as scrap." He doesn't look up yet.

choices.label: What's the most valuable secret you've found?

nodes.text: He finishes the blade before answering -- he never starts a fight, but he's always ready to finish one, and answering too fast feels the same to him. "Something worth the cost of my past, maybe. I'm trying to stop accumulating regrets before the price gets any higher." He doesn't say what it was.

outcome.value: Cutter digs Breach wreckage for secrets worth more than scrap. Holds something back, deliberately.
